items.title: Netflix announces Icebreaker, the latest hockey romance based on a bestselling novel
items.summary_auto: Netflix announced Monday that it’s adapting the bestselling book Icebreaker by English author Hannah Grace into a “college romance drama series." The young adult romance novel, published in 2022, is about an ambitious figure skater, Anastasia Allen, and a hunky hockey team captain, Nate Hawkins, who fall in love after they’re forced to share the ice.

items.title: Indigenous language interpreter at Ontario legislature remembered for tireless advocacy
items.summary_auto: An Anishininiimowin interpreter at Queen’s Park is being remembered for his work as an advocate for land, water and language in northwestern Ontario. John Cutfeet was a member of Kitchenuhmaykoosib Inninuwug (KI). He died unexpectedly on June 9 at 65 years old, prompting condolences from First Nation leaders and organizations all over social media. Here's some of what's been shared about him since his passing.

items.title: Justice minister says new bail and sentencing law is a 'major step forward'
items.summary_auto: The Liberal government's long-promised reforms to Canada's bail and sentencing legislation are now law. Bill C-14, theThe Bail and Sentencing Reform Act, received royal assent on Monday. The new law amends the Criminal Code, the Youth Criminal Justice Act and the National Defence Act to tighten bail and sentencing across all three.

items.title: Winnipeg judge removes high-risk label for man found not criminally responsible in parents' killings
items.summary_auto: A Winnipeg man found not criminally responsible for killing his parents and stabbing a woman during a 2021 psychotic episode has successfully had a designation labelling him high-risk removed.

items.title: N.L. cocaine case stopped after trial scheduled to end 7 days too late
items.summary_auto: Drug-related charges have been halted against a western Newfoundland man because the scheduled end of his trial was seven days past guidelines laid out by Canada’s top court.

items.title: Canada's 'completely nuts' tax code could complicate automated filing plan: watchdog
items.summary_auto: Taxpayers' Ombudsperson François Boileau is pushing for simplified, automated tax filing as his office faces its highest number of complaints in three years.

items.title: FBI says it foiled a plot to attack UFC event at White House
items.summary_auto: Law enforcement officials disrupted a planned attack targeting the UFC show staged at the White House this past weekend, according to court papers unsealed Tuesday and a social media post from FBI Director Kash Patel.

items.title: Toy Story 5 is the best sequel yet. It's still barely about kids
items.summary_auto: Toy Story 5 is a surprisingly strong entry in a franchise that two movies ago struggled to justify returning to its plush, plastic and porcelain characters. Though it’s undercut by the fact that like virtually every other children’s film nowadays, Toy Story is hardly about kids at all.

items.title: Country rock star Jelly Roll and podcaster wife, Bunnie Xo, file for divorce
items.summary_auto: American rapper and singer Jelly Roll has filed for divorce from his wife, podcaster Bunnie XO, citing "irreconcilable differences." Jelly Roll had credited his wife for helping him in his recovery from addiction and crime.

items.title: E-scooter injuries rising among children, B.C. hospital data shows
items.summary_auto: E-scooter-related injuries among children are on the rise, according to new data from B.C. Children’s Hospital, as the devices grow in popularity across the province.

items.title: CBC will no longer show NHL games as sublicense with rights-holder Rogers Sportsnet ends
items.summary_auto: The NHL will no longer be shown on CBC after it and national rights-holder Rogers Sportsnet were unable to come to agreement on a new sublicensing deal that would have allowed the public broadcaster to air games on its Saturday program Hockey Night in Canada.

items.title: Competition Bureau to examine Canada's food supply chain
items.summary_auto: The Competition Bureau is looking to investigate how competition along the food supply chain affects grocery prices for Canadian consumers.

items.title: Singer Bonnie Tyler faces long road to recovery after emerging from induced coma
items.summary_auto: Singer Bonnie Tyler is no longer in a coma but remains "very unwell" in intensive care, an update on her official website says. The Grammy-nominated pop star, 75, was hospitalized last month on Faro, Portugal, where she lives, for emergency intestinal surgery. She was later placed in an induced coma.

items.title: Iran says Israel must withdraw from Lebanon to sign tentative deal to end war with U.S.
items.summary_auto: Iran's top diplomat said Tuesday that the tentative deal to end the war with the United States would require Israel to withdraw from Lebanon — a condition Israel has already rejected and that could sink the agreement, leading to the resumption of all-out war.

items.title: Senate report, wildfire researcher push for federal emergency management agency
items.summary_auto: A wildfire researcher is urging the federal government to create an emergency management agency in the wake of a Senate report on forest fires.

items.title: Liberal government tables First Nations clean drinking water legislation
items.summary_auto: Indigenous Services Minister Mandy Gull-Masty has tabled the Liberal government's long-awaited legislation to govern clean drinking water in First Nations communities.

items.title: Tornado touches down in northwestern Alberta village, damaging homes and businesses
items.summary_auto: A tornado touched down in northwestern Alberta last night, pulling trees from the earth and ripping roofs from homes in the village of Girouxville.

items.title: Conservatives pitch legalizing some psychedelics with bill to revamp Canada's drug laws
items.summary_auto: Conservative MP Corey Tochor introduced legislation on Tuesday to legalize prescribing psychedelics like psilocybin, which researchers and patients say can effectively treat some mental health disorders.

items.title: Hot mic moment at G7 catches Carney, Trump talking about Chinese EVs
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney may not have a bilateral meeting planned yet with U.S. President Donald Trump, but a hot microphone caught them talking about trade at the G7 leaders' summit in Évian-les-Bains, France.

items.title: Zelenskyy, G7 leaders work to persuade Trump 'tide is turning for Ukraine'
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Donald Trump said on ‌Tuesday that Russia should make a peace deal with Ukraine, adding that he would do what he could to end the war after a "very good" meeting between President Volodymyr Zelenskyy and G7 leaders at their summit in France.

items.title: May home sales down 5.1%, but CREA sees positive momentum
items.summary_auto: Despite year-over-year drop, activity was up 5.5 per cent on a seasonally adjusted basis compared with April this year. Prices have also begun to stabilize as buyers and sellers increasingly see eye to eye, according to CREA's senior economist

items.title: Serena and Venus Williams to play doubles together at Wimbledon
items.summary_auto: Serena and Venus Williams are bringing their doubles partnership back at Wimbledon. The All England Club announced a doubles wild card invitation on Tuesday for the sisters to the tournament which starts in less than two weeks.

items.title: Risks of conflict, disease rise when homeless encampments encroach on urban coyote habitat, study cautions
items.summary_auto: Potential risks to human and animal health rise when people experiencing homelessness are forced to seek shelter in the same secluded, inner-city landscapes as urban coyotes, a new study cautions.

items.title: Summer flights to Europe from Halifax up 40% as fewer Canadians choose U.S. for travel
items.summary_auto: Flights to Europe from Halifax are up 40 per cent for this summer over this time last year, which is significantly higher than other airports across North America.

items.title: How sheep may make new apple orchards more environmentally friendly
items.summary_auto: Researchers in Kentville, N.S., are studying ways to establish new orchards in a way that relies less on machinery and chemicals — and more on four-legged assistants. The CBC's Frances Willick has the story.

items.title: We now know smooth green snakes still exist on P.E.I. — but is their population growing?
items.summary_auto: Prince Edward Islanders are again being asked to keep an eye out for smooth green snakes. They're the smallest of P.E.I. 's three snake species — and also the province's rarest reptile.

items.title: Labrador’s 1st Innu nurse receives honorary doctor of laws
items.summary_auto: Memorial University’s Labrador Campus hosted a historic convocation and recognized the decades of contribution made by Labrador’s first Innu nurse.

items.title: Some Canadians are crowdfunding their way through the cost of living crisis
items.summary_auto: Faced with rising costs for necessities such as rent, groceries and utilities, more Canadians are turning to GoFundMe to help make ends meet.

items.title: Bell, Telus — and maybe Rogers — under fire for charging fees that 'appear' to violate new rules
items.summary_auto: Bell and Telus are facing scrutiny from Canada’s telecom regulator for introducing wireless fees the regulator says appear to violate new federal regulations.

items.title: World Zionist Organization removed from Canada's West Bank sanctions draft before release: sources
items.summary_auto: An organization that works with the Israeli government to help with the expansion of settlements in the occupied West Bank was on a draft list of entities Ottawa was set to sanction, CBC News has learned.

items.title: In good news for coral reefs, scientists identify where the toughest ones are
items.summary_auto: New research shows more than 150,000 square kilometres of coral reefs are resilient to the effects of climate change, far more than previously thought.

items.title: More countries are pushing for youth social media bans. Is the world reaching a tipping point?
items.summary_auto: With an increasing number of countries considering or legislating social media bans for young users, there's a growing sense among experts that these restrictions meant to improve online safety are becoming more common and could represent a major reckoning for tech giants.

items.title: Why honeybees are now living in Ontario's legislature
items.summary_auto: Honeybees are now living in wooden houses on the roof of Queen's Park. CBC’s Lorenda Reddekopp spoke with Speaker Donna Skelly — the driving force behind the initiative — about how and why it came together.

items.title: Uruguay's Maxi Araujo scores late equalizer in 1-1 draw with Saudi Arabia
items.summary_auto: Abdulelah Al-Amri scored on a rebound in the 41st minute for Saudi Arabia, and Uruguay's Maxi Araujo responded with an equalizer in the 80th as the teams played to a 1-1 draw in their World Cup opener on Monday.
